author: The Works of John Cassian
book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
Chapter XIII. How the devil always retained this notion of Christ's Divinity (because of His secret working which he experienced) even up to His Cross and Death.

ccel.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

title: Chapter XIII. How the devil always retained this notion of Christ's Divinity (because of His secret working which he experienced) even up to His Cross and Death.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: But perhaps he afterwards ceased and rested, and when his temptations were vanquished laid aside his suspicion because he found no result?

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Nay, it rather remained always in him, and even up to the very cross of the Lord the suspicion lasted in him and was increased by peculiar terrors.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: What need is there of anything further?

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Not even then did he cease to think of Him as the Son of God, after that he knew that such licence was granted to His persecutors against Him.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: But the crafty foe saw even in the midst of His bodily sufferings the signs of Divinity, and though he would have much preferred Him to be a (mere) man, was yet forced to suspect that He was God: for though he would have preferred to believe what he wanted, yet he was driven by surest proofs to that which he feared.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And no wonder: for although he beheld Him spitted on, and scourged, and disgraced, and led to the Cross, yet he saw Divine powers abounding even in the midst of the indignities and wrongs; when the veil of the temple is rent, when the sun hides itself, the day is darkened, and all things feel the effects of the Passion: all things even, which know not God, acknowledge the work of Deity.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And therefore the devil seeing this, and trembling, tried in every way to arrive at the knowledge of His Godhead, even at the very death of the manhood, saying in the person of those who crucified Him: “If He be the Son of God, let Him come down now from the Cross, and we will believe Him.”

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: He certainty perceived that by His bodily Passion our Lord God was working out the redemption of man’s salvation, and also that by it he was being destroyed and subdued, while we were being redeemed and saved.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And so the enemy of mankind wanted by every means and every wile to defeat that which he knew was being done for the redemption of all men.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: “If,” he says, “He be the Son of God, let Him come down now from the Cross and we will believe Him:” on purpose that the Lord might be moved by the reproach of the words, and destroy the mystery, while He avenged the wrong.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: You see then that the Lord even when hanging on the Cross was termed the Son of God.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: You see that they suspect the fact to which they refer.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: And so do you learn, as I said above, even from His persecutors, even from the devil, to believe on the Son of God.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: Who ever came up to the unbelief of the devil?

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: He suspected that He was the Son of God even when He endured death.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: You deny it even when He has risen.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: He suspected that He was God, from whom He hid Himself.

verses.book: The Seven Books of John Cassian on the Incarnation of the Lord, Against Nestorius.
verses.text: You, to whom He has proved it, deny it.
